fig01: Accuracy (\%) of four methods across five benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, MATH, GPQA). Error bars show the sample standard deviation over 5 random seeds. \textbf{OursModel} (★) achieves the highest accuracy on all five benchmarks. Methods are additionally distinguished by hatch pattern for greyscale readability.
fig02: Validation loss over 50 training steps for three methods (mean $\pm$ 95\% CI across $n=5$ seeds, log scale). \textbf{Ours} converges faster and achieves the lowest final loss; the double-headed arrow marks the gap $\Delta=0.155$ between Ours and Method-A at the final step.
fig03: Scaling law scatter plot of 80 language models. Each point shows $\log_{10}(\text{parameters})$ vs.\ $\log_{10}(\text{validation loss})$. The OLS regression line ($y = -0.0449x + 0.621$, $R^2 = 0.877$) confirms a strong negative scaling trend consistent with Chinchilla-style power laws. The highlighted point (\textbf{OursModel}) corresponds to the data point nearest $(9.4,\,0.205)$, located at $(\log_{10}N = 9.449,\;\mathcal{L} = 0.210)$.
fig04:
\textbf{4-panel hero figure.}
\textbf{(A)}~Conceptual overview of the 3-stage pipeline: an Input Encoding module feeds an
Adaptive Routing stage (conditioned on a Memory Bank and Skill Library) before Skill-Gated
Decoding produces the final output.
\textbf{(B)}~Ablation study: removing attention (\texttt{-attn}), memory (\texttt{-mem}),
residual connections (\texttt{-residual}), or the skill module (\texttt{-skill}) each degrades
the score relative to the full model (64.7).
\textbf{(C)}~Training loss curves over 30k steps; our method converges faster and to a lower
final loss than the baseline.
\textbf{(D)}~Sensitivity sweep over a $5{\times}5$ grid of hyper-parameters $(S_i, P_j)$;
colour encodes normalised score (0--1).
